At DeepGram, I started taking everything I had learned along the way of hiring editors from publications, teaching them how to, like, think about the right opportunities, and doing, like, the whole thing and building distribution.

It's like a huge sign.

so that's been a lot of like how we built a company as well is through education and building a community.

SEO, you know, vendor agency consultant that just gives you a recipe of what to do or like, oh, you should do this or that, but don't actually do the work.

think of this as like right now it's internal to us and our customers, our employees that are part of the delivery team.

What we found was the context engineering is one of the most critical things you need to do.

platform, you have is essentially like a context-aware assistant that can pull these artifacts as well, right?

And so then we built a knowledge base as well, so this is a knowledge graph, so think of it as like GraphRag, where as we're doing deep research on ingesting docs, like we can store it in a place where we can retrieve it from, it can also be a place of grounding so we can fact check against it, you know, so let's say ingesting all their docs or their sitemap or something like that, and with a knowledge graph, like you're just able to have a better semantic retrieval, but there's a lot under the hood that we don't need to go into, but just know that's like, we're ingesting a lot of that as we're doing the work, and then I think the core of what we're doing, in addition to having the analytics, the signals, ingesting everything, is really how we go about doing the work, right?
So under the hood, this is kind of our execution layer, if you will, and so under the hood, what we built is a workflow engine as code.
And so what that means is, like, instead of having a dragon,

Drop using like NAN or whatever that is unmaintainable, we have a coding agent that writes in a way, file, excuse me, a file system that writes everything as code, which is way easier to maintain, to run, and to learn from.
So think of it as like, imagine you have an output, and a human puts five comments there.
How could you possibly take those five comments from a Google Doc, and then go in NAN or something like that, and completely rewrite all your workflows based on that input and the calibration, right?
It would be impossible.

But when you're using cloud code or a coding agent plus a framework, you can actually ingest all these signals, and then rewrite everything, and tweak everything, and learn from it, and have evals in the loop as well, right?
So you can see like, when everything is in code, it's all language, right?
Like it's just the code is the best abstraction there is.
Like why use UI as a shittier abstraction to something that?

It's a, this one's an example of one of our agentic workflows where it's generating a draft and then it's running through kind of this loop where it's running evaluations based on a bunch of things we've set like roundness, writing guidelines, and a bunch of direction acceptance.
But it's ingesting a lot of those inputs from the artifacts as well, right?
In addition to doing retrieval and grabbing the research briefs and things like that.
And so it's looping through and self-improving, but then like you can continue to raise the bar of these outputs as well along the way, right?

And delivering for these companies.

I think it's the best fact tracker on the planet.

And for each passage, it comes up with essentially like what the past or not.

And if it's not, it suggests a rewrite, even if it's a nuanced thing, you know.
And so those are ways that we're scaling and building more leverage all the way to like generating the images.

to do a workflow around ingredients review, another one to annotate every medical claim so she could review it as well.

And how does it work as far as onboarding, you new clients?
Seems like you have a bunch of different industries you serve.
How does the onboarding pilot, you know, process work to show them how to use the backend?
Yeah, yeah, great question.
So the onboarding model is we actually changed quite a bit.
starting in June, we shifted to this eight-week strategy sprint.
So the way to think about it is instead of calling it kind of like a pilot or a 90-day opt-out period, which is what we were doing before, we switched to this eight-week strategy sprint.
And since then, knock on low, we had 100% conversion rate.
And so the way it works is like very, very methodical.
We took all...
They're our best people and they're part of this kind of strategy sprint team.
And so we do a kickoff.
We have like every single week we're getting the right input, setting up the workspace.

We are creating all the artifacts, setting their strategy, calibrating the content, calibrating on the definition of done and the definition of quality and then scaling that and getting to quality signals.
And then they can fire us, we can fire them by week seven essentially is kind of how we set it up.
So that gives us like a mutual guarantee that it's like, it's a mutual fit and we feel good about it, you know.
And then from there, it's based on work streams.
So think of work streams as like lanes of execution.
So it can be editorial, programmatic, landing pages.
It could be, you know, newsletter, social.
Those are all different types of work streams, if you will, right?
Content refresh.
refresh and, and so, and then there, each work stream costs anywhere from nine to.

18,000, and a lot of it is just based on how we calibrate during the strategy spread on the complexity, how many layers of review it's required, you know, and how hands-on we have to be with other aspects.
And, you know, and so that determines essentially like Sentinel-1 is a different level of complexity than, you know, RAM, you know, or Biologica, you know, is a much higher level of complexity.
So either the volume goes down a little bit, or, you know, we charge more for that work stream.
But it's like, it's working really, really well.
And that way, people are not like tied to a certain number.
Oh, we must do this number of outputs.
And it's more like outcome focused, you know.

Yeah, yeah, that's a great, great point.
So, the way we kind of think about it is, right now, we have to validate how to...
And the best way to do that is starting with the kinds of brands and projects that would build trust with the rest of the world, right?

So if we're working with Lovable and Document Code and Webflow and Ramp and all of those, everybody else is like, I want to work with them because they're clear enough to serve these kinds of brands, right?

But then in the process of doing that, the platform is getting better and it's getting to a point where it can start to evolve to being a dashboard to eventually an expert marketplace plus an OS, right?

So the way to kind of think about it is like, today, if you just threw somebody into the platform, no matter how perfect the platform is, they just wouldn't be successful because they don't have the right inputs and the strategy either, right?
But over time, I think as things get better and we are confident that the bar of our internal forward deploy people to actually use the platform is getting easier and easier, right?
Then at that point, you can start to open up the platform more and more.
But I think it's more similar to a Palantir model, except our deal velocity is insane, right?
Like, we're not losing money from the beginning.

Like, Palantir's model was, like, you do a $100,000, $200,000 pilot, lose an F-ton of money, forward deploy seven people, and then eventually it's, like, a recurring multimillion-dollar contract, and you leave a little bit of platform behind, and over time, less and less people behind, you know?
For us, it's really, like, we're an extension of their team, and we're meeting with them 30 minutes a week.
But that actually brings, like, upsells.

Like, so Augment started with one workstream, now they're in four workstreams.
And so they're just coming to us.
Instead of hiring, they're just like, let's just add another workstream.
This is better than a headcount, you know?
And, but then, like, long-term, like, the goal is ultimately to the infrapot part be more of a, for lack of a better word.

called output.ai.
We haven't launched it yet, but all the infrastructure is there.
We're really just kind of, and that's one of the reasons why we're thinking about potentially raising is just to double, like, essentially get even more aggressive about our engineering investments.

I think the part, too, that's a little bit different about kind of how we approach this is, like, revenue is our product usage in some ways, you know, and so, like, we were profitable and didn't raise until March of this year, so we hadn't raised a single dollar.
And so, really, like, even our engineering team wasn't fully in place until, like, April, May, right?
And so, in a lot of ways, like, our revenue is very much, like, representative of, like, the traction, but also, like, we need to double down.
I think a lot of companies, the way they build, it's more like...
Like, let me go do $5 to $10 million seed, pre-seed, seed, whatever, burn all that money, and then figure out product-market fit, and then scale the tech, right, or the go-to-market.

We started by validating the go-to-market, validating everything else, being profitable, using the profits to build, and then raising money once we were kind of like already at a really confident place.
But because of that, obviously, like, we've only burned $3 million today as a company, you know, to get to $11 million.
And so, like, we, there's a lot of room for us to double down, and we just hired first AE four weeks ago, and he already closed 10 deals.

to another agency at all, ever.
